name: Supraland
short_description: Try the demo! A mix between Portal, Zelda and Metroid. Exploration, puzzles, terrible combat, secret upgrades and new abilities that help you reach new places. Playtime 12-25h.
detailed_description:
Supraland is a First-Person Metroidvania Puzzle game by David Münnich from Germany. The main sources of inspiration are Zelda, Metroid and Portal.
Supraland assumes that you are intelligent and lets you play independently. The story is minimal, gives you an overarching goal to pursue, and then sets you free. Despite child friendly visuals, the game targets experienced players. Playtime: ~12-25h


You explore a large interconnected world in which most ways are at first unpassable until you find new abilities to overcome those obstacles. A cornerstone of Supraland's design was to create abilities that are so versatile, they will keep on surprising you by how many different usages they have. If you combine your abilities, the possibilities become even bigger.





Most of the game is about exploring the sandbox world to track down secrets. Often you will think you are about to get out of bounds and beat the level designer, but right there is a chest waiting for you with a very rewarding upgrade. Supraland respects your lifetime and doesn't bloat the playtime with unlimited no-brainer collectibles.



The deeper you get into the game the more you will be facing creative puzzles you've never seen anywhere before, encouraging you to stop and think about what abilities you have and how you can use them in yet another way. Puzzle types that I already knew from other games were immediately rejected in the design process. And it's important to me that once you understand the idea behind a puzzle, you can pretty much immediately solve it instead of having to go through a cumbersome, frustrating execution.



The fighting mechanics are inspired by old-school, fast-paced shooter games like Unreal, Doom and Quake, encouraging high-speed strafing and jumping while throwing shot after shot at hordes of charging enemies without ever having to worry about weapons reloading.


In the demo you can play the first big section of the game and decide if it's for you. The demo progress continues into the full version; you don't need to play anything twice!

supported_languages: English, French, Italian, German, Portuguese - Portugal, Portuguese - Brazil, Finnish, Russian, Japanese, Traditional Chinese, Simplified Chinese, Spanish - Spain, Turkish, Hungarian, Polish, Korean

reviews.review_text:
A mix between Portal, Zelda, and Metroid?  Oh yeah, this is DEFINITELY what it's advertised as and it is SO worth it for that alone.

I played this years ago, but I got bad motion sickness from it at the time and put it down.  I played for maybe 1-2 hours, but didn't think it was that gripping; therefore, I put it more or less out of my mind.  Then I saw it in the "Play Next" section of my library, and a sudden urge to play Supraland ignited within me.  I booted it up and *mostly* finished it over the weekend.

And when leaving the game, I recognized that, while not a masterpiece, it has that same masterclass design that makes Portal, Zelda, and Metroid so special from a game-mechanics perspective.

The different gadgets and puzzles definitely harkened back to all three of the aforementioned games: I love how big the main puzzles were, because it gave me major Zelda vibes.  The Metroidvania part with going around the world to access past places with new abilities was nailed excellently.  Finally, the mechanics of the puzzles definitely had the same structure that a lot of puzzles in Portal had; though I might call it an in-between from this game and The Talos Principal.

The linearity is something I also greatly appreciated because it made the map more manageable and less confusing.  I miss that from Zelda, actually; Breath of the Wild and Tears of the Kingdom are great games, but they did lose their identity with throwing linearity out the window.  Supraland, fortunately, kept a good leash on it, not as tight as a typical Metroidvania, but just the right amount that harkened me back to Ocarina of Time.

None of the puzzles were too difficult, yet challenging enough to get your noggin joggin'.  I love the intuitive nature of the puzzle design as well, as if the way the game wants you to think progresses as it shows you how to use each tool.  And the plethora of hidden chests and stuff to find is AWESOME; game designers who plan to make games with exploration need to take notes from this because filling your map to the tippy top with secrets and things to find, that actually HELP the player, is an essential part of good game design.

The tools and abilities, as well, felt fantastic to have and use, and it progressively changed the way you viewed the world you found yourself in; I'd say this is Supraland's biggest strength. It just feels fun and great to play.

But again, not a masterpiece.  

For one, the graphics aren't great, even for 2019.  I get it, it's indie, it's an older game, it's going for a toy-like look; all those things, very much valid, yet still persistently an issue nonetheless.  Lots of the lighting in the game is really bad, and textures look muddy, even on higher-end settings; it would look fine on Xbox 360/PS3 or as an Xbox One/PS4 Launch title, but this game is coming out at the end of the lifetime for Xbox One/PS4, it could've been better.  Also, the human boy?  My goodness, it almost looks as bad as Andy from the first Toy Story, but at least Andy looks like he has a soul in that potato of a face.

Another thing that left me disappointed and also baffled, considering the Zelda/Metroid inspiration, was that there was virtually one boss and no more.  The campaign felt robbed of having climactic boss moments that would have elevated the different areas you went to, as well as giving the player more opportunities to use their arsenals in more unique ways. 

And while we're on the subject of areas, the game, while having a very unique identity, could've benefited from areas that had maybe just a little more variety?  Thing is, it's in a sandbox, so, yeah, expect lots of sand and rocky formations... But then if this is a sandbox, how in the Supra-HECK is there a volcano?  It could've had more variety.

The soundtrack also kind of sucked, not gonna lie, I felt like a first person adventure game like this does NOT need the same OST vibes as Golf With Your Friends or generic DND music.

While I liked much of the humor, the story was also a bit meh and had blasphemous parts in it (Mostly at the very end).  I'm a Christian and I want fellow Christian gamers to know there's some offensive material that makes light of the nature and character of the Christ.  Is that a reason not to play?  Maybe, obviously pray, consult the scriptures, and don't play if you feel convicted by the Holy Spirit; I personally concluded that I would not throw the baby out with the bathwater and just say "Yeah, the designers were wrong to put that in, but the game is fantastic otherwise.

Also, there is no excuse for this game to not be playable on the Steam Deck, yet here we are, in the year of our Lord, 2026, the game has raked in wads of cash and raked in rave reviews, yet David Münnich can't take a break from eating schnitzels while planning WW3 or whatever the heck Germans do and fix that?  Just not right.

Still with all the gripes, I love this game, and I highly recommend it, sale or not.  In the interest of saving money, Supraland does go on deep discounts during major sales, so your best time to grab it would be then.  Supraland is SupraGOOD.

[h1]Rating: 7/10 -Decent[/h1]

Supraland is an open world metroidvania puzzle game. It's set in a garden and you play as a small little red figurine. Stones and grass look huge. Redtown has ran out of water and it seems like the blue figurines have sabotaged your water pipeline. You now have to get to blue ville and talk to the blue king. That is easier said than done. Pathways are often blocked by something that requires a certain gear. The game leads you naturally to new gear and upgrades. you can also buy upgrades with coins you collect throughout the game. The game has also a bit of combat. There are collectibles that are blocked off in the beginning. You can later get those after obtaining more gear like in a metrovania. The game feels like an adventure. The world is varied and has a lot to offer. If you like open world puzzle adventure games with metrovania elements, then superland is for you.
Starts off slow, but the depth starts to really shine after a little while. There's a lot more to this game that initially appears. Highly recommend for First-Person Metrovania / Puzzle fans.
